audit_scope: Identify whether eval-barrier reduction is the right intervention for the AX-vs-mlx_lm Gemma 4 direct-decode gap, per the G1 artifact's primary recommendation.
method: Enumerated explicit `mlx_sys::eval(...)` callsites in ax-engine-mlx hot path (not test code, not embedding-only paths).
Cross-referenced with sample profile counts from `gemma_ax_sample.txt` and `gemma_mlxlm_sample.txt`.
Checked `.shape()` / `.data_f32()` / `.item()` call sites — these are the implicit-eval triggers.

explicit_eval_callsites_in_hot_path.purpose: Single decode step. ONE eval per token bundling logits + collect_eval_refs (KV cache backing buffers). Comment in source notes this is intentionally batched.
N-gram speculative decode verification. Bundles predicted_arr + KV refs.
explicit_eval_callsites_in_hot_path.per_step_count: 0 in direct-decode workload (n-gram off when max_output<64)

explicit_eval_callsites_in_hot_path.purpose: Linear-attention n-gram commit. Bundles logits + KV refs.
explicit_eval_callsites_in_hot_path.note: Gemma 4 E2B does not use linear attention

explicit_eval_callsites_in_hot_path.purpose: Forces evaluation of q_f32 before CPU-side data_f32() access. Inside TurboQuant decode path.
explicit_eval_callsites_in_hot_path.note: Not hit by Gemma 4 baseline (TurboQuant gated)

implicit_eval_triggers_in_forward_path.note: `MlxArray::shape()` does NOT trigger evaluation — confirmed by inline comment at model.rs:1094 (`shape metadata is available without eval`). All 80 `.shape()` calls are eval-free.
implicit_eval_triggers_in_forward_path.verdict: No implicit-eval triggers in the per-token forward path on the Gemma 4 baseline.

sample_profile_finding.interpretation: The 33-sample gap represents ~0.026 ms per decode token in extra `mlx::core::gpu::eval` time. This is small (~3% of the ~0.8 ms per-token wall in AX). Eval barriers are NOT the primary bottleneck.

real_gap_location.primary_finding: The structural gap is in MlxArray wrapper construction, not eval barriers.
interpretation: Every intermediate tensor in AX's Rust forward path constructs an `MlxArray` wrapper. mlx_lm's Python forward composes through nn.Module which probably allocates fewer transient wrappers.
Each MlxArray destructor + reconstruction triggers allocator activity (`MetalAllocator::malloc`).

Gemma 4-specific architecture adds many transient ops per layer (per_layer_input gating: 6 ops × 32 layers = 192/forward; layer_scalar multiply: 32/forward; q_norm + k_norm + attn_post_norm: ~3 ops × 32 layers = 96/forward).
Combined: AX's Rust forward creates ~300+ extra transient MlxArray objects per Gemma 4 forward pass that mlx_lm's Python forward does not.

audit_conclusion: Eval-barrier reduction (Intervention B from G1 artifact) is the WRONG primary intervention. The eval count is already minimal (1 per single_decode step, already batched with KV refs) and the gap to mlx_lm is small (~3% of decode wall). The actual lever is Intervention A (forward-path allocation audit) — specifically targeting Gemma 4's per-layer-input gating, layer_scalar, and the q_norm/k_norm/attn_post_norm operations which collectively account for ~300 extra MlxArray instances per forward pass.

revised_recommendation.primary: Intervention A: forward-path allocation audit, targeted at Gemma 4 per-layer operations

candidate_changes_to_investigate.potential: Check if gate * pli + multiply + qw + rms_norm + add can be folded into fewer MLX ops. mlx_lm's gemma3n.py uses an `altup`/`laurel` module structure that may compose into fewer underlying ops via mlx.nn module fusion.

Could be folded into an adjacent operation (e.g. multiply by layer_scalar inside the rms_norm scale combination, or fused with the post-FFN add). One MLX op per layer × 32 layers = 32 fewer ops per forward pass if eliminated.

candidate_changes_to_investigate.site: Attention norm chain (attn_norm + attn_post_norm + q_norm + k_norm)
candidate_changes_to_investigate.potential: RMSNorm has known fusion patterns with adjacent matmuls. mlx_lm may emit fewer norm-related transient arrays through nn.RMSNorm reuse.

candidate_changes_to_investigate.site: Cross-cutting: Rust wrapper overhead vs Python nn.Module
candidate_changes_to_investigate.scope: Investigate whether mlx_sys::MlxArray Rust wrapper has higher per-instance overhead than Python mlx.core.array — e.g. extra ref-count manipulation, extra Dtype copies.
candidate_changes_to_investigate.potential: If yes, a wrapper-level optimisation (move semantics, shared dtype constants) would benefit ALL model families, not just Gemma 4.

non_goal_in_intervention_A: Do not change eval-barrier policy — the audit shows current batching at single_decode_with_turboquant_context is already optimal.
Do not change KV cache layout — the per-decode-step eval already bundles KV refs.

next_step: Open Intervention A: forward-path allocation audit. Estimated 1-2 weeks. First concrete deliverable: per-callsite MlxArray construction count for one Gemma 4 forward pass, comparing against the same instrumented counters added to mlx_lm.
